content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Tech nical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
Lar ry Sage 
Administration 
322-5660 
Schneider, Collinson & Lange 
120 Richardson Street 
Grass Valley, CA 
95945 
Dear Mr. Sage: 
Executive/legal 
32?-5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
October 27, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. A-83-220 
This letter is in response to your request for advice on the 
conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
In seeking our advice, you asked us to assume that the following 
facts are correct. 11 
1. 
You are an attorney who specializes in education law. 
In 1980, you were hired by the Nevada County Counsel's 
Office under an agreement between Nevada County and 12 
school districts. 
The school districts agreed to fund 
your County position for three years in exchange for 
your service s .ll 
2. 
During the fall and early winter of 1982, you 
discussed with the County and the school districts the 
possibility of working solely as the school districts' 
attornjY once the present contract ended on June 30, 
1983.2. 
II The events are in chronological order. 
~I You were a Nevada County civil service employee. 
11 
From the time of your initial interview for your 
position with the County Counsel's Office, the possibility that 
the job would lead to a position as in-house counsel for the 
school districts, or private practice, was discussed. 


Lar ry Sage 
October 27, 1983 
Page 2 
3. 
Several months before the contract's expiration, a 
committee of three school superintendents met with you 
to discuss the terms of a possible new contract between 
the districts and the County for your services. 
4. 
You decided to run for a judgeship and you applied for 
other legal positions. 
At this time, you informed the 
school districts that you planned to leave the Nevada 
County Counsel's Office. 
You eventually associated 
with the firm of Schneider, Collinson & Lange. 
5. 
Prior to the contract's expiration, some of the school 
districts indicated that they preferred to retain your 
services, even if you were in private practice. 
At 
their request, you submitted a proposal which was 
prepared at the law offices of Schneider, Collinson & 
Lange. 
6. 
The contract expired on June 30, 1983, and you 
continued working for the County Counsel's Office and 
the school districts under an oral, month-to-month 
arrangement until the school districts decided how they 
wanted their legal services provided. 
7. 
The school districts' committee for legal services 
negotiations did a staff study of all of the available 
options for providing legal services to the district. 
The study they prepared included your proposal. 
8. 
You told the school districts that you were unable to 
advise them on your proposal and, instead, they had 
their labor attorney, Mr. Stroup, review it. 
He made 
some changes and recommendations which the school 
district committee incorporated into their proposal to 
you. 
The County had all of the proposals reviewed by 
the Acting County Counsel, James Curtis. 
9. 
You began private practice in the Schneider law offices 
and 9 out of the 12 school districts have decided to 
contract with you for services. 
You are waiting to 
sign the contract until after you have heard from us. 
Your question is whether the proposed contract, or the way 
it was negotiated, violates the provisions of the Act. 
You 
included with your letter a copy of an Attorney General's 
Opinion (66 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 156 (1983» 
and asked whether 
its findings concerning the Political Reform Act are applicable 
in the present situation. 


Larry Sage 
October 27, 1983 
Page 3 
DISCUSSION 
Government Code Section 87100il prohibits a public 
official from making, participating in the making, or in any way 
attempting to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know 
that he has a financial interest. 
An official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on: 
* * * 
(c) 
Any source of income ••• aggregating $250 or 
more in value provided to ••• the official within 12 
months prior to the time when the decision is made •••• 
(Section 87103 (c) .) 
If a decision's effect will have an impact on an official's 
own income, the affect is considered to be "material" if it will 
directly increase or decrease the amount of income to be 
received by the official in an amount of $100 or more. 
(2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Sect ion 18702 (b) (3) (A) • ) 
In the present case, the 
decision to award the legal services contract to you was a 
decision which will foreseeably have a material financial effect 
on you because it will increase your income by $100 or more. 
The question is whether you made, participated in the making of, 
or used your position to influence, the decision concerning the 
awarding of the contract. 
You did not "make" the decision to award the contract as 
this decision was made by the school districts. 
(See the 
enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(b).) 
You also 
did not "participate in the making of the decision." 
An 
official participates in decision making when, acting within the 
authority of his position, he: 
(1) 
Negotiates, without significant substantive 
review, with a governmental entity or private person 
regarding the decision; or 
il Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 


Larry Sage 
October 27, 1983 
Page 4 
(2) 
Advises or makes recommendations to the 
decision-maker, either directly or without significant 
intervening substantive review, by: 
(A) 
Conducting research or making any 
investigation which requires the exercise of 
judgment on the part of the official or designated 
employee and the purpose of which is to influence 
the decision; or 
(B) 
Preparing or presenting any report, 
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which 
requires the exercise of judgment on the part of 
the official or designated employee and the 
purpose of which is to influence the decision. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18700(c) (1) I 
(2) (A) and (B).) 
The activities that you engaged in did not constitute these 
types of activities. 
At the school districts' request, you 
submitted a proposal and then counterproposals were exchanged. 
You did not advise the school districts regarding the proposals 
or the contract. 
Any negotiations that you engaged in were 
substantively reviewed by the attorneys for the school districts 
and the County.2/ 
The present situation is substantially different from the 
situation in the Attorney General's Opinion that you attached to 
your letter. 
(660ps. Cal. Atty. Gen. 156 (1983).) 
In that 
Opinion, the Attorney General found that several public 
officials violated the provisions of the Act by "participating" 
in the making of a contract in which they had a financial 
interest. 
Their activities included proposing the contract; 
2/ 
In July 1983, at the school districts' request, you 
reviewed an opinion, written in 1978 by the then-County Counsel, 
Brian Bishop, identifying the types of legal services that the 
County Counsel's Office is required by law to provide to school 
districts which do not retain legal counsel. 
Although you 
prepared and submitted your own written opinion to the school 
districts, you stated that you did not believe, and do not now 
believe, that the opinion had any impact on the decision to 
award you the contract. 
In requesting the opinion, the school 
districts stated only that they "wanted to see if the law had 
changed. " 


Lar ry Sage 
October 27, 1983 
Page 5 
advising the county as to much of the detail of the contract 
(e.g. what it should cover; the definition of the scope of 
services); and making suggestions and comments to the county 
officials charged with investigating the feasibility of the 
plan. 
You did not engage in any of these types of activities. 
You also did not "use your official position to influence" 
the decision regarding the awarding of the contract. 
Regulation 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(e) defines this as furthering or 
attempting to affect in any manner any decision: 
(1) 
Within or before his or her agency; or 
(2) 
Before any agency which is appointed by or subject 
to the budgetary control of his or her agency. 
You were an employee of the County Counsel's Office and the 
decision on the contract was made by the school districts. 
The 
school districts are separate and not subject to your agency's 
budgetary control. 
Thus, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(e) does 
not apply. 
Furthermore, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(f) 
specifically excludes "actions by public ••• employees ••• 
relating to their compensation or the terms or conditions of 
their employment or contract" from the definition of "using your 
position to influence." 
Based on the above analysis, and assuming the facts to be 
correct, nothing in the proposed contract, or the way it was 
negotiated, violates the provisions of the Political Reform 
Act. 
You indicated on the telephone that your local District 
Attorney, John Darlington, is considering the possible 
application of Section 1090. 
If I can be of any further help to you, please feel free to 
contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
JSM:plh 
Enclosure 
Very truly yours, 
:Jan£!; Shank::/.tt~(/ 
Janis Shank McLean 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


Mr. Robert Blasier, Esquire 
Division Chief, Enforcement Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Re: 
Determination of any Conflict of Interest 
Dear Sir: 
After a short discussion with Mr. John Darlington, our 
District Attorney, he suggested that I submit to you the 
question of the existance of any conflict of interest in my 
conduct. 
I am currently a deputy county counsel with Nevada County, 
and have resigned my position effective September 30, 1983. 
The following facts are submitted for use in your 
determination. 
1 ask that you assume these facts to be true 
as submitted so that you can reach your decision as soon as 
possible. 
A separate sheet is attached containing the 
addresses and phone numbers of all persons mentioned herein 
if you decide to confirm the facts presented. 
The County and twelve school districts executed a three year 
contract to provide a school attorney as a deputy county 
counsel. The schools paid the County and the Office of the 
County Counsel was to hire a new employee. 
Some two months 
later, I was interviewed for the job. 
During the initial 
interview (1 went through two, each approximately one year 
apart), it was discussed that the position could lead to 
eventual private practice (as I was then in private practice) 
or even to that of an in-house counsel as an employee of the 
districts, like our neighbor Place County did with a former 
deputy county counsel, Doug Lewis. 
In other words, the 
flexibility of options available was to be a drawing card. 
However, in all aspects, the deputy county counsel was to be, 
and in fact is, a County employee governed by the County 
civil service system, and is not in any wayan employee of 
the school di stri cts. 
Further, in late fall, early winter, of 1982, I held 
discussions with the Nevada County Superintendent of Schools, 
Jerome F. Hund and County Counsel, Brian A. Bishop as to the 
feasibility of letting the school districts have me, as an 


attorney, housed in the County Superintendents Office when 
the County attorney agreement expired. 
Some months before the County agreemeent was to expire on 
June 30, 1983, a committee of three school superintendents 
met with me to discuss the terms of a possible new contract 
between the districts and the County for my services. 
This 
occurred almost simultaneously with my application for the 
Grass Valley Judicial District Judgeship. 
At the time of the 
Public announcement of the Judge candidates, including me, a 
number of school district superintendents approached me to 
determine my intentions of remaining with the office of the 
County Counsel and as to my future availability to be the 
school districtsr-attorney in any capacity. including private 
pra~tice. 
I informed a number of superintendents that I would not 
remain in the County Counsel's office, irrespective of the 
judgeship selection. 
I was selected among the final three 
candidates by the Judicial Council appointed committee, and 
tried for the judgeship vigorously. 
This included favorable 
endorsements from all elected officials of this County at 
State and Federal levels. 
I also applied for the position of 
County Counsel with Placer County and for the schools 
attorney position with E1 Dorado County Superintendent of 
Schools. 
The superintendents informed of my decision to 
leave the office of the County Counsel were, among others, 
Donna Soldano, Irwin Karp, Jim Stewart, and Dr. Mike 
Barkhurst. 
I then proceeded to interview with various attorneys and 
firms in Nevada County. 
I held discussions and meetings with 
Erse1 Edwards, Linda Hartman, Richard Schneider, Mara1ee 
Ne1der-Adams, Bob Eli, Gary Gordon, Ray Shine, Scott Brown, 
and Craig Diamond. 
I eventually decided to associate, 
without any name added to the firm. with Schneider, Collinson. 
& Lange. 
During this period, and prior to the County agreement 
terminating on June 30, 1983, it was suggested by some 
superintendents that they would prefer to retain my services 
as attorney, even if in private practice. 
Those who 
indicated these preferences were Dr. Mike Barkhurst, Ty 
Blount, and Irwin Karp. 
At this time there were still no terms agreed upon by the 
superintendents committee for a new contract with the County. 
A major disagreement arose between the superintendents over 
the evaluation method used for my last evaluation. 
As a 
result of no new terms being agreed upon, the contract 
expired by its own terms with the County on June. 30, 1983. 
I 
have remained as a deputy county counsel and the school 


districts orally agreed to continue on a monthly basis as 
before until they decided how they want their attorney 
services provided. 
As a result of school district politics, a new committee 
composition for legal services negotiations was created. 
It 
consisted of Jim Stewart, Ty Bount and Steve Halterman. 
They 
performed a staff study including my response to their 
solicted proposal for private practice. 
They studied and 
analyzed all available options for providing legal services 
to the school districts. 
My proposal was discussed and compiled with the assistance of 
Richard Schneider. 
It was prepared and totally done at the 
law offices of Richard Schneider. 
I advised the school districts committee of a possible 
appearance of conflict of interest if I were to advise them 
with regard to my proposal which I have never done. 
I 
advised them to have an independent counsel review my 
proposal and advise them accordingly. 
The school districts 
used their labor attorney, Mr. Bob Stroup. 
Mr. Stroup, I am 
informed, made some changes and recommendations which the 
school district committee incorporated into their proposal to 
me. 
My proposal was a direct response to a school district 
committee request to submit a proposal when the districts 
learned of my decision to leave the County Counsel IS Office. 
The County has had all proposals reviewed by James Curtis, 
acting County Counsel. 
I advised the schools that the Education Code required any 
contract with an attorney to be reviewed by the County ---
Counsel. 
I advised them I could not and would not perform 
such a review and that it had to be done by James Curtis, 
acting County Counsel. 
Copies of all proposals from Mr. 
Schneiderls office were given to James Curtis, acting County 
Counsel. 
The school districts, I am informed, communicated 
directly with Mr. Curtis. 
I was so informed by Dr. Mike 
Barkhurst. 
At no time was I ever an employee of the school districts. 
At no time did I ever advise the County of Nevada or anyone 
as to the effects of the private practice proposal. 
At no 
time did I ever advise any school district or any of their 
employees as to the effects of the private practice proposal. 
For your information the costs paid to the County by the 
school districts under the attorney services agreement for 
last year was approximately $41,000.00 to $42,000.00. 
This 
private practice proposal was for 5 percent less than paid 
the last year. 
In other words, each district would pay 5 
percent less, or a total of $39,000.00. 


As of this date, there exists no contract for legal services 
between the school districts and Nevada County, none between 
the school districts and myself, none whatsoever exists or 
has been signed by anyone. 
Negotiations are still ongoing 
and school boards will be asked to act in the immediate 
future. 
I am informed that 9 of the 12 school superintendents will 
recommend to their boards to retain me as their attorney, and 
3 of the superintendents are recommending another 
arrangement, primarily to contract with the County. 
I am led 
to believe they (the 3) are negotiating with the County 
currently. 
lastly, I have extensively reviewed the latest opinion of the 
Attorney General on the subject. 
A copy is enclosed for your 
assistance. 
I offer the following facts with regard to that 
opinion: 
(I) 
I am not employed by the school districts, but am 
employed by the County. The school districts were independent 
contractors with the County for legal services. 
That 
contract expired by its own terms. 
(2) 
The request for a proposal based on my being in 
private practice was requested and initiated by the school 
districts. 
This very option, however, as well as others 
involving my employment directly by the schools was discussed 
from my first interviews and occasionally over the last 
years. 
(3) 
The proposal for private practice was prepared with 
Richard Schneider and made, at all times, by the offices of 
Schneider, Collinson & lange. 
(4) 
At no time have I ever advised my employer, the 
County, as to the effects or benefits of the private practice-
pro",osal. 
(5) 
At no time have I ever advised any school district 
as to the effects or benefits of the private practice 
proposal. 
R,espectful1y, 
LARRY G. SAGE 
Deputy County Counsel 


•

(916) 322·5662 
Lar ry Sage

Statements of Economic In!erest 
322-6444 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political 
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an 
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
adv 
e request, you may contact this attorney directly at 
(916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex 
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to 
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21 
workii1g days. 
BAM:plh 
Enclosure 
Very truly yours, /[ . 
ILU ~AJ 
Barbara A. Milman 
Chief, Legal Division

sections.conclusion: Based on the above analysis, and assuming the facts to be correct, nothing in the proposed contract, or the way it was negotiated, violates the provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 87100il prohibits a public 
official from making, participating in the making, or in any way 
attempting to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know 
that he has a financial interest. 
An official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on:
sections.question_synthetic: Does a proposed contract between school districts and an attorney (formerly employed as a deputy county counsel) violate the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions, either in the contract terms or in the negotiation process?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The proposed contract does not violate the Political Reform Act because the attorney did not make, participate in making, or use his official position to influence the school districts' decision to award the contract. His submission of a proposal and participation in substantively reviewed negotiations did not constitute prohibited participation in decision-making.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: ocr, old; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter and the FPPC response letter (A-83-220). Extracted information focuses on the formal advice letter response from Janis Shank McLean dated October 27, 1983. OCR quality is generally good with minor formatting artifacts. The document includes detailed legal analysis of Government Code Section 87100 and related regulations.

conduct. 
I am currently a deputy county counsel with Nevada County, 
and have resigned my position effective September 30, 1983. 
The following facts are submitted for use in your 
determination. 
1 ask that you assume these facts to be true 
as submitted so that you can reach your decision as soon as 
possible. 
A separate sheet is attached containing the 
addresses and phone numbers of all persons mentioned herein 
if you decide to confirm the facts presented. 
The County and twelve school districts executed a three year 
contract to provide a school attorney as a deputy county 
counsel. The schools paid the County and the Office of the 
County Counsel was to hire a new employee. 
Some two months 
later, I was interviewed for the job. 
During the initial 
interview (1 went through two, each approximately one year 
apart), it was discussed that the position could lead to 
eventual private practice (as I was then in private practice) 
or even to that of an in-house counsel as an employee of the 
districts, like our neighbor Place County did with a former 
deputy county counsel, Doug Lewis. 
In other words, the 
flexibility of options available was to be a drawing card. 
However, in all aspects, the deputy county counsel was to be, 
and in fact is, a County employee governed by the County 
civil service system, and is not in any wayan employee of 
the school di stri cts. 
Further, in late fall, early winter, of 1982, I held 
discussions with the Nevada County Superintendent of Schools, 
Jerome F. Hund and County Counsel, Brian A. Bishop as to the 
feasibility of letting the school districts have me, as an 


attorney, housed in the County Superintendents Office when 
the County attorney agreement expired. 
Some months before the County agreemeent was to expire on 
June 30, 1983, a committee of three school superintendents 
met with me to discuss the terms of a possible new contract 
between the districts and the County for my services. 
This 
occurred almost simultaneously with my application for the 
Grass Valley Judicial District Judgeship. 
At the time of the 
Public announcement of the Judge candidates, including me, a 
number of school district superintendents approached me to 
determine my intentions of remaining with the office of the 
County Counsel and as to my future availability to be the 
school districtsr-attorney in any capacity. including private 
pra~tice. 
I informed a number of superintendents that I would not 
remain in the County Counsel's office, irrespective of the 
judgeship selection. 
I was selected among the final three 
candidates by the Judicial Council appointed committee, and 
tried for the judgeship vigorously. 
This included favorable 
endorsements from all elected officials of this County at 
State and Federal levels. 
I also applied for the position of 
County Counsel with Placer County and for the schools 
attorney position with E1 Dorado County Superintendent of 
Schools. 
The superintendents informed of my decision to 
leave the office of the County Counsel were, among others, 
Donna Soldano, Irwin Karp, Jim Stewart, and Dr. Mike 
Barkhurst. 
I then proceeded to interview with various attorneys and 
firms in Nevada County. 
I held discussions and meetings with 
Erse1 Edwards, Linda Hartman, Richard Schneider, Mara1ee 
Ne1der-Adams, Bob Eli, Gary Gordon, Ray Shine, Scott Brown, 
and Craig Diamond. 
I eventually decided to associate, 
without any name added to the firm. with Schneider, Collinson. 
& Lange. 
During this period, and prior to the County agreement 
terminating on June 30, 1983, it was suggested by some 
superintendents that they would prefer to retain my services 
as attorney, even if in private practice. 
Those who 
indicated these preferences were Dr. Mike Barkhurst, Ty 
Blount, and Irwin Karp. 
At this time there were still no terms agreed upon by the 
superintendents committee for a new contract with the County. 
A major disagreement arose between the superintendents over 
the evaluation method used for my last evaluation. 
As a 
result of no new terms being agreed upon, the contract 
expired by its own terms with the County on June. 30, 1983. 
I 
have remained as a deputy county counsel and the school 


districts orally agreed to continue on a monthly basis as 
before until they decided how they want their attorney 
services provided. 
As a result of school district politics, a new committee 
composition for legal services negotiations was created. 
It 
consisted of Jim Stewart, Ty Bount and Steve Halterman. 
They 
performed a staff study including my response to their 
solicted proposal for private practice. 
They studied and 
analyzed all available options for providing legal services 
to the school districts. 
My proposal was discussed and compiled with the assistance of 
Richard Schneider. 
It was prepared and totally done at the 
law offices of Richard Schneider. 
I advised the school districts committee of a possible 
appearance of conflict of interest if I were to advise them 
with regard to my proposal which I have never done. 
I 
advised them to have an independent counsel review my 
proposal and advise them accordingly. 
The school districts 
used their labor attorney, Mr. Bob Stroup. 
Mr. Stroup, I am 
informed, made some changes and recommendations which the 
school district committee incorporated into their proposal to 
me. 
My proposal was a direct response to a school district 
committee request to submit a proposal when the districts 
learned of my decision to leave the County Counsel IS Office. 
The County has had all proposals reviewed by James Curtis, 
acting County Counsel. 
I advised the schools that the Education Code required any 
contract with an attorney to be reviewed by the County ---
Counsel. 
I advised them I could not and would not perform 
such a review and that it had to be done by James Curtis, 
acting County Counsel. 
Copies of all proposals from Mr. 
Schneiderls office were given to James Curtis, acting County 
Counsel. 
The school districts, I am informed, communicated 
directly with Mr. Curtis. 
I was so informed by Dr. Mike 
Barkhurst. 
At no time was I ever an employee of the school districts. 
At no time did I ever advise the County of Nevada or anyone 
as to the effects of the private practice proposal. 
At no 
time did I ever advise any school district or any of their 
employees as to the effects of the private practice proposal. 
For your information the costs paid to the County by the 
school districts under the attorney services agreement for 
last year was approximately $41,000.00 to $42,000.00. 
This 
private practice proposal was for 5 percent less than paid 
the last year. 
In other words, each district would pay 5 
percent less, or a total of $39,000.00. 


As of this date, there exists no contract for legal services 
between the school districts and Nevada County, none between 
the school districts and myself, none whatsoever exists or 
has been signed by anyone. 
Negotiations are still ongoing 
and school boards will be asked to act in the immediate 
future. 
I am informed that 9 of the 12 school superintendents will 
recommend to their boards to retain me as their attorney, and 
3 of the superintendents are recommending another 
arrangement, primarily to contract with the County. 
I am led 
to believe they (the 3) are negotiating with the County 
currently. 
lastly, I have extensively reviewed the latest opinion of the 
Attorney General on the subject. 
A copy is enclosed for your 
assistance. 
I offer the following facts with regard to that 
opinion: 
(I) 
I am not employed by the school districts, but am 
employed by the County. The school districts were independent 
contractors with the County for legal services. 
That 
contract expired by its own terms. 
(2) 
The request for a proposal based on my being in 
private practice was requested and initiated by the school 
districts. 
This very option, however, as well as others 
involving my employment directly by the schools was discussed 
from my first interviews and occasionally over the last 
years. 
(3) 
The proposal for private practice was prepared with 
Richard Schneider and made, at all times, by the offices of 
Schneider, Collinson & lange. 
(4) 
At no time have I ever advised my employer, the 
County, as to the effects or benefits of the private practice-
pro",osal. 
(5) 
At no time have I ever advised any school district 
as to the effects or benefits of the private practice 
proposal. 
R,espectful1y, 
LARRY G. SAGE 
Deputy County Counsel

Your letter requesting advice under the Political 
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an 
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political 
Practices Commission.

CONCLUSION: Based on the above analysis, and assuming the facts to be correct, nothing in the proposed contract, or the way it was negotiated, violates the provisions of the Political Reform Act.

In seeking our advice, you asked us to assume that the following facts are correct. 11 1. You are an attorney who specializes in education law. In 1980, you were hired by the Nevada County Counsel's Office under an agreement between Nevada County and 12 school districts. The school districts agreed to fund your County position for three years in exchange for your service s .ll 2. During the fall and early winter of 1982, you discussed with the County and the school districts the possibility of working solely as the school districts' attornjY once the present contract ended on June 30, 1983.2. II The events are in chronological order. ~I You were a Nevada County civil service employee. 11 From the time of your initial interview for your position with the County Counsel's Office, the possibility that the job would lead to a position as in-house counsel for the school districts, or private practice, was discussed. Lar ry Sage October 27, 1983 Page 2 3. Several months before the contract's expiration, a committee of three school superintendents met with you to discuss the terms of a possible new contract between the districts and the County for your services. 4. You decided to run for a judgeship and you applied for other legal positions. At this time, you informed the school districts that you planned to leave the Nevada County Counsel's Office. You eventually associated with the firm of Schneider, Collinson & Lange. 5. Prior to the contract's expiration, some of the school districts indicated that they preferred to retain your services, even if you were in private practice. At their request, you submitted a proposal which was prepared at the law offices of Schneider, Collinson & Lange. 6. The contract expired on June 30, 1983, and you continued working for the County Counsel's Office and the school districts under an oral, month-to-month arrangement until the school districts decided how they wanted their legal services provided. 7. The school districts' committee for legal services negotiations did a staff study of all of the available options for providing legal services to the district. The study they prepared included your proposal. 8. You told the school districts that you were unable to advise them on your proposal and, instead, they had their labor attorney, Mr. Stroup, review it. He made some changes and recommendations which the school district committee incorporated into their proposal to you. The County had all of the proposals reviewed by the Acting County Counsel, James Curtis. 9. You began private practice in the
embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter A-83-220 addresses whether an attorney's transition from deputy county counsel to private practice contracting with school districts violates the Political Reform Act. The FPPC concludes that no violation occurred because the attorney did not make, participate in, or improperly influence the school districts' contracting decision.
